foundation.title: STRUCT
foundation.subtitle: Custom Records
foundation.content: A Struct allows you to cluster different data types (int, char, float) into a single logical 'Record' or object.
foundation.scenario: A Student ID Card. It holds a Photo (Binary), a Name (Text), and a Number (Integer) simultaneously.

examples.title: Creation
examples.explanation: Defining a blueprint for a new record type using 'struct'.
examples.code: struct Player { int hp; char id; };

examples.title: Accessing
examples.explanation: Using a dot ('.') to see exactly one thing from inside the record.

examples.title: Naming
examples.explanation: Using 'typedef' to give your record a short name without saying 'struct' every time.

foundation.keyPoint: struct allows multi-type containers in C.

foundation.title: FILES
foundation.subtitle: Disk I/O
foundation.content: C uses 'File Pointers' to read/write to your hard drive. You must manually open and close these connections.
foundation.scenario: A Physical File Cabinet. You open it (fopen), put a new paper inside (fprintf), and then CLOSE the door (fclose).

examples.title: Opening
examples.explanation: Creating a link to a file on your disk using a special 'FILE' pointer.

examples.title: Writing
examples.explanation: Pouring text into the file stream directly using the fprintf() function.

examples.title: The Close
examples.explanation: Releasing the file so other apps can see it.

foundation.keyPoint: Always check if f is NULL after opening.

foundation.title: HEADER
foundation.subtitle: Includes
foundation.content: C uses Header files (.h) to share logic between different .c files in a large system.
foundation.scenario: A Shared Recipe Book. Every chef (file) can include (include) the recipes (logic) in the .h file instantly.

examples.title: Standard
examples.explanation: Importing a box of pre-made tools from the official C compiler library.

examples.title: Local One
examples.explanation: Including a file you wrote yourself in the same folder using quotes.

examples.title: The Guards
examples.explanation: Adding special lines starting with # to prevent C from reading the same file twice.
